summary:
Stilicho (c. 359–408 AD) was the last great military defender of the Western Roman Empire — a half-Vandal, half-Roman general who served as military commander (magister militum) and effectively regent for the weak Western emperor Honorius, and who twice defeated Alaric's Visigothic invasions of Italy (395–396 and 401–403 AD) and repelled the invasion of the usurper Radagaisus (405–406 AD). His military skill, diplomatic acumen, and personal commitment to defending Rome were the primary reasons the empire survived as long as it did in the west.

Stilicho's story is a tragedy of imperial politics: despite his military successes and his devotion to the empire, his half-barbarian origin made him vulnerable to court intrigue. His enemies convinced the paranoid emperor Honorius that Stilicho was conspiring with Alaric — an accusation that may have been stimulated by Stilicho's controversial willingness to negotiate with Alaric rather than always fighting him. In 408 AD, Stilicho was arrested and executed without trial. The Roman soldiers who had served under him were killed, and their barbarian wives and children were massacred — driving many of their relatives straight into Alaric's army. Two years later, in 410 AD, Alaric sacked Rome ([[event:2033|Sack of Rome by Visigoths]]). Stilicho's execution directly caused the catastrophe his life had been spent preventing — a bitter illustration of how political paranoia can destroy the very competence it depends upon.

figures.name: Quintus Aurelius Symmachus
figures.summary: Roman senator, orator and author (345–402 CE)
